docs.title: Research Lab Wiki
docs.text:
Self-directed, fully-cited investigations — each one traced to peer-reviewed studies and official sources, weighed honestly, and closed with an evidence-graded verdict.

  • BPA in Thermal Receipts & Human Health

    How bisphenol A from till and card receipts gets into the body, what it does, and how real the risk is — for the average person versus cashiers. 29 cited studies, evidence-graded.

    Read the research

  • Collagen: What It Actually Does vs What People Think

    Sleep, gut, skin, joints, bone, muscle — which collagen claims are real, and which are just a glycine effect. 74 cited studies, funding-flagged.

    Read the research

  • Testosterone: What Actually Raises It vs What People Think

    Diet, sleep, supplements, no-fap, seed oils, sun, plastics — which "boost testosterone naturally" claims hold up, and which (soy, pomegranate, no-fap, sun-on-testicles) are myths. 96 cited studies, evidence-graded.

    Read the research

  • Seed Oils: What They Actually Do vs What People Think

    Inflammation, heart disease, obesity, "toxic" frying oil — which "seed oils are poison" claims hold up, and which (the inflammation mechanism, "cause cancer," "swap to tallow") are myths or marketing. 96 cited sources, all PMIDs verified.

    Read the research

  • Creatine: What It Actually Does vs What People Think

    Kidney damage, hair loss, "it's a steroid," bloating, must-cycle — which creatine fears hold up (almost none), and what it actually does for muscle, brain and health. The most-studied, best-evidenced, safest sports supplement. 119 cited sources, all PMIDs verified.

    Read the research

  • Methodology

    How every research here grades evidence, states confidence, and verifies each citation before it is published.

    How this is graded

Unlisted

A public link, but excluded from search engines (noindex). Citations point only to public, independently verifiable sources (DOIs / official agencies). Informational only, not medical advice. Available in English and Ελληνικά — use the language selector, top right.

docs.title: BPA in Thermal Receipts and Human Health
docs.text:
How bisphenol A from till and credit-card receipts gets into the body, what it does to human health, and how real and how large the risk is — for the average person versus cashiers. This is the whole picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence holds — settled exposure facts first, the genuinely-contested disease-causation question last.

Complete · Published 25 June 2026 · Rebuilt 14 July 2026 — every cited study re-read from source and re-verified · 29 cited studies & official assessments · Confidence: High on exposure · Moderate on harm magnitude.

TL;DR

Thermal receipts are coated in free, rub-off BPA (≈19.6 mg/g, in 44% of receipts) that absorbs through the skin — a route that bypasses liver detox, so more reaches the blood in its active form and lingers for days. Hand sanitizer amplifies absorption up to 185×. Diet is still the average person's main BPA source, so a stray receipt is a small, easily-avoided add-on — but for cashiers it's a real occupational exposure (~2–3× higher urinary BPA, with DNA-oxidative-damage and insulin-resistance correlates). "BPA-free" receipts are usually BPS/BPF — no safer. EFSA (2023) cut the safe dose ~20,000× and says background diet already exceeds it; the US FDA still calls it safe and doesn't regulate receipts at all. Verdict: avoidance is cheap and rational — strongly so for cashiers and pregnant women.

The picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence holds

  • Tier 1 — settled (multi-study + agency): receipts carry mg-level free rub-off BPA;1 gloves abolish the exposure (proof it's the skin);23 the dermal route is more bioactive + slower-clearing than diet;4 cashiers run ~2–3× elevated across three cohorts;563 "BPA-free" = BPS/BPF, as hormonally active as BPA;7 EFSA cut the safe dose ~20,000× and diet already exceeds it.10
  • Tier 2 — well-supported: hand-sanitizer amplifies absorption (~up to 185×);1 one worst-case event ≈ the population 95th percentile;1 BPA lowers sperm + bioactive androgen (18-study meta);12 diet/canned food dominates for the average person (so a stray receipt is a small add-on);1413 the EU restricts receipts, the US does not.1618
  • Tier 3 — consistent but observational: low-dose organ effects (CLARITY academic);19 prenatal hyperactivity (presumed hazard);20 a modest CVD signal;21 BPS/BPF obesity/diabetes epidemiology;24 placental cytotoxicity in vitro.26
  • Tier 4 — genuinely contested: whether the receipt fraction causes a specific disease turns on the unresolved EFSA-vs-FDA/BfR dispute.27 Verdict: real exposure, contested harm, cheap avoidance — precaution is rational, strongly for cashiers and pregnant women.

docs.title: Tier 1 — Settled: the exposure is real
docs.text:
Bisphenol A (BPA) is a high-volume industrial chemical and a confirmed endocrine disruptor — it binds estrogen receptors and acts as a weak exogenous estrogen, with additional anti-androgenic and metabolic activity. In thermal receipt paper it is not a bound polymer (as in polycarbonate plastic or epoxy can-linings) but a free, powdery colour developer coating the surface — so it rubs off onto skin on contact.

Receipts carry milligram quantities of free BPA. In a US survey of 50 thermal receipts, 22 (44%) carried high BPA at 19.6 ± 1.0 mg per gram of paper (≈1–2% of the paper's weight) — thousands of times more free BPA than migrates from a plastic bottle; BPS, the main substitute, was found in 52% of receipts.1

Gloves abolish the exposure — the cleanest proof it's the skin. Holding receipts for 2 hours without gloves raised volunteers' urinary BPA from 1.8 → 5.8 µg/L (peak 11.1 at 8 h); nitrile gloves abolished the rise entirely,2 and a separate cashier intervention found the ~2× post-shift rise vanished with gloves.3

The skin route bypasses first-pass detoxification — more bioactive, slower-clearing. Dermal BPA shows prolonged kinetics: after a single contact, urinary excretion rose linearly for ~2 days, was still detectable in half of participants after 1 week (up to 9 days in one), with a higher unconjugated (bioactive) fraction than diet — whereas swallowed BPA clears within 24 h.4

Cashiers absorb most of their BPA dermally, and run ~2–3× elevated. In cashiers, dermal contact contributed 51.9–84% (geometric mean 70.9%) of total urinary BPA;5 French cashiers had median total urinary BPA 8.92 µg/L vs 3.54 µg/L in controls,6 and Korean female cashiers showed ~2× higher post-shift urinary BPA.3 A documented occupational exposure across three independent cohorts.

"BPA-free" usually means BPS/BPF — not safer. A systematic review (1,370 studies screened) found BPS and BPF are "as hormonally active as BPA," with estrogenic, anti-estrogenic, androgenic and anti-androgenic activity (mean estrogenic potency relative to BPA: BPF 1.07, BPS 0.32).7 Worse for the dermal route, more free (unmetabolized) BPS than BPA survives in urine after handling receipts (6.9% vs 2.7%) because BPS resists metabolism.8 Only genuinely bisphenol-free paper (e.g. Pergafast 201) is a real fix — proven feasible when Switzerland mandated it (BPA-developed receipts fell 82.2% → 10.8%).9

EFSA cut the safe daily dose ~20,000× — and diet already exceeds it. In 2023 EFSA set a tolerable daily intake of 0.2 ng/kg body weight/day — ~20,000× below its 2015 temporary 4 µg/kg — on immune-system (Th17) evidence, concluding mean and high dietary exposure in all age groups exceed the new TDI by two-to-three orders of magnitude.10 Receipts add a dermal, bioactive increment on top of an already-exceeded background.

docs.title: Tier 2 — Well-supported (amplifiers, magnitude, hormones, diet, regulation)
docs.text:
Hand sanitizer and greasy hands are powerful amplifiers. Penetration enhancers in sanitizers/lotions open the skin barrier: after sanitizer use, up to 185× more BPA transferred from a receipt to a wet hand than a dry hand.1 In the worst-case protocol (sanitizer → hold receipt → eat finger-food), unconjugated serum BPA peaked at ~7 ng/mL and urinary total BPA hit ~20 µg/g creatinine within 90 min — pushing a person to roughly the NHANES 95th percentile (~11 µg/g) of the whole US population from a single event.1

Putting the 20,000× in receipts. Worst-case occupational handling (~10 h/day) models to a dermal BPA dose of ~71 µg/day (≈0.97 µg/kg bw/day).11 Hold that exposure fixed and only move the regulatory line: it is ~0.24× the 2015 temporary TDI — Biedermann's 2010 paper literally called it "42× below" the limit of its day — but ~4,850× the 2023 TDI.10 The receipt didn't change; EFSA's ~20,000× cut did. (Illustrative order-of-magnitude; the absorbed fraction is smaller.)

BPA lowers sperm count and bioactive androgen. A 2024 systematic review/meta-analysis of 18 studies found urinary BPA inversely associated with sperm concentration (β −0.03) and total count (β −0.05), alongside higher SHBG, higher estradiol, and lower bioactive androgen — a coherent estrogenic/anti-androgenic signature (the below-reference sperm-quality link was "not robust").12

Diet dominates for the average person — and it's well-quantified. BPA is essentially absent from unprocessed food; it migrates out of epoxy can-linings and plastic, rising sharply with heat, sunlight and storage.13 Canned tuna reached up to ~105 µg/kg (highest after retort at 121 °C; the lining shed up to 646 µg/kg into a fatty simulant);14 canned sardines carried 5.2–48.7 µg/kg, migration +437% with temperature.15 So for the general public a stray receipt is a smaller add-on to the dietary baseline — it's cashiers where the dermal route dominates.

The EU restricts receipt BPA; the US does not. Commission Regulation (EU) 2016/2235 banned BPA in thermal paper above 0.02% from 2 January 2020,16 ECHA lists BPA as an endocrine-disruptor SVHC,17 and the EU banned BPA in all food-contact materials in 2024;18 the US FDA does not regulate thermal receipts at all, so there is no US receipt limit and US exposure is ~1,000× EFSA's new safe level.18

docs.title: Tier 3 — Consistent but observational (disease associations)
docs.text:
Important caveat

Almost all human disease epidemiology measures total urinary BPA (mostly dietary), not receipt-specific BPA. These studies establish what BPA at population exposure is associated with; receipts are one contributor. Causal attribution of a specific disease to receipt-borne BPA specifically is not established, and most associations are cross-sectional.

Low-dose organ effects (CLARITY-BPA academic arm). The academic laboratories reported consistent low-dose effects at 2.5 / 25 / 250 µg/kg/day across brain, heart, prostate, ovary, mammary and immune tissue, "at doses relevant to human exposures," with non-monotonic dose–response — why "no effect at high dose" does not guarantee safety at realistic low doses.19

Prenatal / childhood — the precautionary core. A systematic review (29 rodent + 3 human studies) concluded "early life BPA exposure is a presumed human hazard for the development of hyperactivity," urging precautionary reduction in pregnant women, infants and children.20

Cardiovascular — a modest, consistent-direction signal. The prospective EPIC-Norfolk study found incident coronary artery disease OR 1.13 (95% CI 1.02–1.24) per SD urinary BPA, attenuating to borderline after full adjustment; earlier NHANES cross-sectional data linked BPA to CVD (OR 1.39) and diabetes.21 The type-2-diabetes signal is present but inconsistent — positive in a prospective Nurses' Health analysis (OR up to 2.08 in middle-aged women) and an overall-positive meta-analysis, but null in older women.2223

BPS/BPF now carry disease-outcome signals + cell toxicity. Human biomonitoring ties urinary BPS/BPF to obesity and type-2 diabetes (BPS the most impactful), though the epidemiology is younger and mostly cross-sectional;2425 and in human placental (JEG-Tox) cells, BPA and BPF triggered more inflammasome/apoptosis caspase activity than BPS, with BPF uniquely activating caspase-8 — direct cytotoxicity in a pregnancy-relevant tissue.26 Cashier BPA also correlated with the DNA-oxidative-damage marker 8-OHdG and with insulin resistance.53

docs.title: Tier 4 — Genuinely contested (disease causation)
docs.text: Whether the receipt fraction causes a specific disease is unproven and expert-contested. EFSA's 20,000× cut rests on an immune (Th17) endpoint that Germany's BfR and a group of regulatory toxicologists argue is not a demonstrated adverse effect — BfR derived a TDI ~1,000× higher;27 the FDA still reads the CLARITY Core study as "no clear adverse effects."1918 Two respected agencies disagree by ~1,000× on how dangerous BPA is at all. This is genuine expert disagreement, not a settled harm.

docs.title: The scorecard
docs.text: Claim Verdict Confidence Receipts carry large amounts of free BPA that transfers to skin True High Dermal route yields more bioactive, longer-lasting BPA than diet True High Hand sanitizer / lotion massively amplifies absorption True High Cashiers have measurably elevated BPA (≈2–3×); gloves prevent it True High Receipts are the main BPA source for the average person False (diet dominates) High "BPA-free" receipts are safer Not established (usually BPS/BPF) High BPA at population exposure harms human health Associated & plausible, mostly cross-sectional & contested Moderate Receipt-borne BPA specifically causes a named disease Unproven Low–Moderate Is avoiding receipts worth it? Yes High (decision)

docs.title: What this rebuild changed
docs.text: This page was rebuilt on 14 July 2026 by re-reading every PMID-bearing study from its source abstract and re-verifying every identifier against PubMed. That process confirmed the entire report and required no corrections — all 15 PMID-bearing sources title-matched (0 wrong PMIDs), and the agency documents (EFSA, FDA, ECHA, EU regulations) are verifiable by DOI/URL. The exposure facts, the "regrettable substitution," and the 20,000× regulatory shift all held on a fresh read. The findings are now presented ranked by how strongly the evidence backs them.

docs.title: Mitigation
docs.text:
Receipt-borne BPA is one of the most easily avoided exposures, and the interventions are evidence-backed (gloves abolished the urinary rise in two independent studies23).

General public: decline the paper receipt / choose digital receipts (the exact tool Dr. Shanna Swan recommends28); don't handle receipts right after hand sanitizer or lotion (the 185× amplifier1); don't let children hold receipts;20 wash hands before eating.1

Cashiers / retail workers (highest exposure): wear (nitrile) gloves — the single most effective, proven control;23 minimize skin time; avoid hand cream/sanitizer immediately before handling; employers should switch to bisphenol-free thermal paper (e.g. Pergafast 201).9

The honest bottom line: the exposure is real and quantified; the disease consequence is plausible but unproven for receipts specifically; avoidance is cheap. Therefore precaution is rational — strongly for cashiers and pregnant women, mildly for everyone else. The case rests on asymmetry of risk (cheap to avoid, plausibly harmful), not on a demonstrated receipt-to-disease causal chain.

Every source below is real and verifiable by its DOI / PMID / URL. Grades: agency = official risk assessment / regulation · SR/MA = systematic review / meta-analysis · primary = human biomonitoring / controlled exposure · secondary = review / commentary.

docs.title: Collagen: What It Actually Does vs What People Think
docs.text:
Collagen is sold for hair, skin and nails and increasingly for sleep, gut health, "detox", glutathione, inflammation, anxiety, arteries and joints. Which of those are real, how strong is the evidence, and are they collagen-specific or just a glycine effect? This is the whole picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence holds — settled first, single-study and debunked last.

Complete · Published 4 July 2026 · Rebuilt 14 July 2026 — every cited study re-read from source and re-verified · 74 cited studies · Confidence: High on the collagen-vs-glycine framing · Moderate on the modest musculoskeletal effects.

TL;DR

Collagen protein is ~⅓ glycine plus proline/hydroxyproline, and an incomplete protein (essentially no tryptophan — don't count it as protein). Trace each benefit to a mechanism and most of the "wellness" claims are GLYCINE effects, not collagen-specific — collagen is a costly glycine delivery vehicle, and plain glycine is cheaper. The popular skin benefit is unproven, not disproven: the pooled positive signal collapses in independent, high-quality trials. Sleep is a modest glycine effect; gut-barrier, "detox", glutathione, anti-inflammatory, anxiety and artery claims are weak-to-unproven for collagen. Where collagen is best-evidenced is musculoskeletal — joint/osteoarthritis pain (a moderate but funding-limited effect, with an independent null), postmenopausal bone density, tendon size with training, and wound healing in patients — all modest, product-specific (UC-II ≠ hydrolyzed peptides ≠ gelatin), and it does not build muscle (it can't raise muscle protein synthesis; whey beats it). The single most reliable pattern across the whole literature is manufacturer funding driving the positive results.

The picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence holds

  • Tier 1 — settled: collagen is ~⅓ glycine + an incomplete protein (no tryptophan, leucine-poor);1 most "wellness" benefits are glycine effects, delivered expensively;4 collagen does NOT build muscle (can't raise muscle protein synthesis — whey and even pea beat it);25 the skin benefit is null once you remove industry-funded and low-quality trials;6 and manufacturer funding is the dominant confound.3
  • Tier 2 — real but modest: joints/OA is collagen's best-evidenced use (pain SMD −0.35, but manufacturer-funded with an independent null);89 sleep is a modest glycine effect (the seed trial missed its primaries, control not protein-matched);123 the glucose-blunting hydrolysate is peptide-specific but single-funder;14 glutathione needs GlyNAC (collagen has no cysteine);16 bone density + tendon size are funded surrogates (structure, not strength).1718
  • Tier 3 — weak/conditional: gut-barrier, anti-inflammatory, wound healing (sick patients / topical dressings), nail/hair, nocturia (a weak glycine bladder-reflex, not ADH), liver, arteries.23
  • Tier 4 — myths: anxiety (dose-impossible extrapolation);24 "rebuilds arteries" (the causal test is null);25 "less urine at night via ADH" (a misconception);23 and a fabricated glycine-sleep citation people circulate.
  • Safety: heavy metals are brand-variable (buy third-party-tested); the kidney-stone risk is hydroxyproline-specific, so plain glycine dodges it.2627

docs.title: Tier 1 — The strongest, settled points
Collagen is ~⅓ glycine and an incomplete protein
docs.text: Collagen is the body's main structural protein, built on a repeating Gly-X-Y triple helix in which glycine is every third residue (~33% by count, ~19% by weight), with proline and hydroxyproline in the X/Y positions.1 It is an incomplete protein: tryptophan is absent, essential-amino-acid content is only ~14%, and it is leucine-poor (~0.9 g per serving vs ~4.3 g in whey, below the ~2.5–3 g needed to trigger muscle protein synthesis), so its protein-quality score (PDCAAS/DIAAS) is near-zero and collagen should not be counted toward daily protein for muscle.12 Eaten as gelatin or hydrolyzed peptides, it is digested to free amino acids plus a small fraction of stable di/tri-peptides (Pro-Hyp, Hyp-Gly) that appear in blood and stimulate fibroblasts in vitro — but it does not reach the skin as collagen, and plasma peptide levels are micromolar and transient, so detection is not efficacy.

docs.title: The load-bearing crux: most benefits are glycine effects
docs.text: Almost every downstream "wellness" effect can be explained by supplying glycine + proline and by collagen being a cheap, glycine-dense protein — not a unique peptide mechanism proven in humans. A 15 g collagen dose delivers only ~3.5 g glycine, exactly the dose used in the glycine sleep trials.3 Where a benefit exists, the parsimonious reading is glycine, delivered expensively — and free glycine is several-fold cheaper per gram.4

docs.title: Collagen does NOT build muscle
docs.text: Two independent tracer trials show that collagen cannot raise muscle protein synthesis: whey (and even pea) stimulate it while collagen does not, because collagen is leucine-poor and incomplete.25 "Fat-free mass" on a body-scan includes connective tissue and water, so a body-comp signal can be real yet not be muscle. For muscle or sarcopenia, a complete protein beats collagen — it should not displace it.

docs.title: The skin benefit collapses under bias control
docs.text: Pooled meta-analyses look positive, but the single independent, bias-stratified review is decisive: in non-industry-funded trials and in high-quality trials the benefit disappears (Myung & Park 2025, 23 RCTs / 1,474 — "no clinical evidence to support collagen supplements to prevent or treat skin aging").6 A newer independent umbrella review (Ravindran 2026) reports the opposite, but it pools mostly low-quality reviews and inherits their bias — two independent reviews disagreeing is itself a sign the primary trials are too weak to settle it.7 Crucially, no skin RCT uses an equal-protein comparator — every one compares collagen to inert placebo, never to isonitrogenous whey — so a general protein effect cannot be ruled out. Verdict: unproven, not disproven. (Nail evidence is one single-arm, open-label, no-placebo study; hair evidence is scarce and industry-adjacent — and nail/hair are keratin, not collagen.)

docs.title: Funding bias is the dominant confound
docs.text: Nearly every positive collagen trial has a collagen manufacturer among the authors (Rousselot, GELITA/VERISOL, Nitta, Jellice, Nippi, Newtree); the benefit disappears in the independent, high-quality subgroups.63 This one pattern explains most of the gap between the marketing and the evidence.

docs.title: Tier 2 — Real but modest (joints, sleep, glucose, glutathione, bone, tendon)
Joints / osteoarthritis — the strongest case
docs.text: A trial-sequential meta-analysis (35 RCTs / 3,165 patients) finds collagen reduces OA pain (SMD −0.35) and improves function (SMD −0.31), and the analysis reached its required information size.8 The catch: effects sit near the minimal-clinically-important line, the trial base is heavily manufacturer-funded, and a manufacturer-independent RCT (N=68) found no benefit over placebo.9 Undenatured type-II collagen (UC-II, 40 mg) is a different product with an oral-tolerance mechanism and its own positive trial — but "beating glucosamine + chondroitin" is a low bar, because that comparator is itself at/near placebo in the large independent GAIT trial, and UC-II's evidence is almost entirely from its maker.1011 No human trial shows cartilage regeneration; every benefit is a symptom score.

docs.title: Sleep — a modest glycine effect, primaries null
docs.text: The whole collagen-sleep case is one small (n=13), manufacturer-funded RCT whose primary sleep outcomes were null; only a secondary "fewer awakenings" (21.3 vs 29.3, p=0.028) and a near-ceiling cognition score improved.3 A decisive flaw compounds this: the comparator was an inert taste-matched drink, not protein-matched, so even "fewer awakenings" can't be pinned on collagen over any bedtime protein. The glycine sleep literature it leans on is real but small and high-risk-of-bias: ~3 g glycine before bed shortens sleep-onset latency via a core-temperature drop.1213

docs.title: The one collagen-peptide-specific signal: post-meal glucose
docs.text: A specific hydrolysate cut the post-meal glucose rise ~40–53% — genuinely collagen-peptide-specific — but every human trial shares one funder (Rousselot), it has not been independently replicated, its GLP-1/gastric-emptying mechanism was measured in mice only, and it is mechanistically a general protein-preload effect that whey shares.1415

docs.title: Glutathione, bone, tendon
docs.text: Glutathione needs GlyNAC — glycine plus N-acetylcysteine — used in aged/diseased people, from a single lab, mostly open-label; collagen supplies no cysteine, so it cannot reproduce it, and the mainstream view is that cysteine, not glycine, is the rate-limiting precursor in the first place.16 Bone: König 2018 (postmenopausal women, 5 g/day, 12 months) found significant spine and femoral-neck density gains — Gelita-funded, a surrogate endpoint (density, not fractures), heterogeneous when pooled.17 Tendon — loading does the work: 5 g specific collagen peptides during 14 weeks of heavy training increased tendon cross-sectional area, but stiffness, strength and performance rose equally in both groups; a 2026 review grades this formally — GRADE A that size/stiffness rise with training, GRADE A against an added strength effect.1819 Muscle gains are real only as generic added protein (small fat-free-mass effect, SMD 0.48, with training), not as a muscle-builder.20

docs.title: Tier 3 — Weak, single-study, or conditional
docs.text:
Gut, inflammation. Gut-barrier "leaky gut" repair is in-vitro/animal only (one mouse study found collagen worsened colitis, and a glutamine gut-permeability meta-analysis is null); anti-inflammatory benefit is confined to diseased cohorts, and the collagen trial's own inflammation markers were null.3 A collagen serving delivers ~3.5 g glycine — roughly 4× below the ~15 g/day that moved markers in the glycine trials.

Wound healing. In clinical nutrition, collagen peptides speed pressure-ulcer healing — a dose-response trial (a high-dipeptide formula beating a low-dipeptide one at equal protein) is the cleanest evidence a benefit is peptide-specific, and an independent RCT found ~2× faster healing.2122 But this is enteral nutrition in malnourished patients, and much of the "collagen heals wounds" evidence people cite is actually topical collagen dressings — a physical scaffold device, a different route.

Nocturia has a direct human signal, but for glycine, not collagen (~6 g/day in overactive-bladder patients), via a bladder/spinal reflex — not antidiuretic hormone and not "deeper sleep".23 Liver "detox" is a real conjugation pathway but not a proven human outcome (and the rodent bile-acid arm doesn't transfer — mouse bile acids are taurine-conjugated). Arteries: human data are small, manufacturer-linked, and surrogate-only.

docs.title: Tier 4 — Myths and misconceptions
docs.text:
Anxiety. No human trial has tested collagen against an anxiety endpoint. The claim extrapolates from 30–60 g isolated glycine in psychiatric trials (mixed-to-null; the largest, CONSIST, was negative) — doses 7–20× the glycine in a collagen serving.24 At food doses this is a misconception.

"Rebuilds arteries." The glycine→heart-disease causal link fails the best genetic and mouse tests (pleiotropy-robust Mendelian randomization and ApoE-knockout feeding, both null);25 digestion does not route dietary collagen to arterial tissue.

"Less urine at night." No trial measured urine volume or ADH, so the "collagen makes you produce less urine at night" claim is a misconception — the real (weak) signal is bladder-signaling, not reduced urine output.23

Product type is everything. UC-II (40 mg, immune tolerance) ≠ hydrolyzed peptides (grams, substrate/signalling) ≠ gelatin + vitamin C (synthesis cofactor) ≠ topical dressings (a device). A trial on one form is not evidence for another — and a widely-circulated "2025 N=48 glycine sleep RCT" does not exist in PubMed; don't cite it.

docs.title: The scorecard
docs.text: Claim Verdict Tier Collagen "is protein" / builds muscle Myth — incomplete protein, can't raise MPS<sup>2</sup><sup>5</sup> 1 Wellness benefits are collagen-specific Mostly a glycine effect (delivered expensively)<sup>4</sup> 1 Skin (hydration/elasticity/wrinkles) Unproven (null in independent + high-quality trials)<sup>6</sup> 1 Joints / osteoarthritis Real but modest (SMD −0.35; funding-limited, independent null)<sup>8</sup><sup>9</sup> 2 Deepens sleep Modest, glycine-driven (collagen primaries null)<sup>12</sup><sup>3</sup> 2 Blunts post-meal glucose Promising (peptide-specific, but single-funder, mouse mechanism)<sup>14</sup> 2 Boosts glutathione Needs GlyNAC — collagen has no cysteine<sup>16</sup> 2 Bone density / tendon size Promising surrogate (funded; structure, not function)<sup>17</sup><sup>18</sup> 2 Gut "leaky gut" / anti-inflammatory Weak/unproven (in-vitro/animal; disease-only)<sup>3</sup> 3 Wound healing Real in patients (clinical nutrition / topical dressings)<sup>21</sup> 3 Nails / hair Weak (one open-label study; keratin, not collagen) 3 Calms anxiety Myth (dose-impossible extrapolation)<sup>24</sup> 4 "Rebuilds arteries" Myth (causal test null)<sup>25</sup> 4 "Less urine at night" (via ADH) Misconception (no urine-volume/ADH data)<sup>23</sup> 4

docs.title: What this rebuild changed
docs.text: This page was rebuilt on 14 July 2026 by re-reading all cited studies from their source abstracts and re-verifying every PMID against PubMed. That process confirmed the entire verdict — the skin-null, glycine-vehicle, doesn't-build-muscle and funding-bias conclusions all held on a fresh read — and corrected only attribution details: all 48 PMID-bearing sources title-matched, 0 wrong PMIDs (the prior verification held), and five first-author names were fixed. The findings are now presented ranked by how strongly the evidence backs them.

docs.title: Dose, cost, and safety
docs.text:
• Cost: if the goal is the glycine benefits, plain glycine (~3 g) is several-fold cheaper per gram of glycine than collagen.4
  • Heavy metals: brand-variable — testing found ~47% of protein powders exceeded a conservative safety threshold. Buy third-party-tested (NSF/USP).26
  • Kidney stones — hydroxyproline-specific. Hydroxyproline (unique to collagen/gelatin) is metabolized to oxalate; gelatin raised urinary oxalate 43% vs whey at 5–10 g, while glycine itself contributes under 5% of urinary oxalate — so the caution applies to collagen, and plain glycine dodges it. A flag for recurrent calcium-oxalate stone-formers, negligible for others with good hydration.27
  • Allergen / pregnancy: marine collagen is a fish allergen (rare anaphylaxis; a serious skin-reaction case report exists); bovine carries a theoretical, undocumented prion caveat; pregnancy safety data are limited — treat as insufficient, not "proven safe".

docs.title: The honest bottom line
docs.text:
Collagen is, first, a glycine/proline-rich incomplete protein. Its most-marketed benefit (skin) is unproven once funding and quality are controlled; its "wellness" benefits are mostly glycine mechanisms extrapolated past the human evidence, and glycine is cheaper bought directly. Its genuinely best-evidenced uses are musculoskeletal — joint/OA pain, bone density, tendon size, and wound healing in patients — all modest, product-specific, and manufacturer-entangled, and it is an inferior protein for muscle. The evidence-based reason to take collagen, if any, is joint or connective-tissue support alongside training — not the wellness claims it is mostly sold on.

This is an independent research summary, evidence-graded and funding-flagged. It is not medical advice. Every claim is footnoted to a primary study or systematic review with a DOI/PMID.

  1. Collagen amino-acid composition (~⅓ glycine by residue; no tryptophan; leucine-poor). Structural biochemistry, DOI 10.1110/ps.062560107 · PMID 17189476. ↩↩↩

  2. Oikawa SY, et al. (2020) Am J Clin Nutr. Whey but not collagen raised muscle protein synthesis (equal-protein, n=22; independent). DOI 10.1093/ajcn/nqz332 · PMID 31919527. ↩↩↩↩

  3. Thomas C, et al. (2023) Eur J Nutr. Collagen peptides pre-sleep RCT (n=13; primary endpoints null; control not protein-matched; Rousselot-funded). DOI 10.1007/s00394-023-03267-w · PMID 37874350. ↩↩↩↩↩↩↩↩

  4. Meléndez-Hevia E, et al. (2009) J Biosci 34(6):853-872. Glycine ~10 g/day synthesis shortfall (conditionally essential). DOI 10.1007/s12038-009-0100-9. ↩↩↩↩

  5. McKendry J, et al. (2024) Am J Clin Nutr. Whey and pea raised myofibrillar synthesis; collagen did not (n=31). DOI 10.1016/j.ajcnut.2024.05.009 · PMID 38762187. ↩↩↩

  6. Myung SK, Park Y (2025) Am J Med 138(9):1264-1277. Systematic review/meta-analysis, 23 RCTs / 1,474; skin benefit null in independent + high-quality subgroups. DOI 10.1016/j.amjmed.2025.04.034 · PMID 40324552. ↩↩↩↩

  7. Ravindran S, Pizzol D, López-Gil JF, et al. (2026) Aesthetic Surg J Open Forum. Umbrella review (16 meta-analyses / 113 RCTs); high-GRADE positive for skin, but pools mostly low-quality reviews. DOI 10.1093/asjof/ojag018. ↩

  8. Liang CW, et al. (2024) Osteoarthritis Cartilage 32(5):574-584. Trial-sequential meta-analysis, 35 RCTs / 3,165; OA pain SMD −0.35, function SMD −0.31. DOI 10.1016/j.joca.2023.12.010 · PMID 38218227. ↩↩↩

  9. Yuenyongviwat V, et al. (2025) Sci Rep. Manufacturer-independent OA RCT, n=68; null vs placebo. DOI 10.1038/s41598-025-17505-0. ↩↩↩

  10. Lugo JP, Saiyed ZM, Lane NE (2016) Nutr J. Undenatured type-II collagen (UC-II, 40 mg) vs glucosamine+chondroitin vs placebo, n=191 (InterHealth-funded). DOI 10.1186/s12937-016-0130-8 · PMID 26822714. ↩

  11. Clegg DO, et al. (GAIT, 2006) N Engl J Med 354:795-808. Glucosamine/chondroitin at/near placebo (N=1,583) — the weak comparator UC-II "beats". DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa052771. ↩

  12. Yamadera W, et al. (2007) Sleep Biol Rhythms. 3 g glycine pre-bed shortens sleep-onset/slow-wave latency. DOI 10.1111/j.1479-8425.2007.00262.x. ↩↩↩

  13. Soh J, et al. (2023) GeroScience. Systematic review of glycine in humans (52 studies); sleep evidence small/high-risk-of-bias. DOI 10.1007/s11357-023-00970-8 · PMID 37851316. ↩

  14. Grasset E, et al. (2024) Food Sci Nutr. Collagen hydrolysate + post-meal glucose RCT (n=16; GLP-1/emptying mechanism measured in mice only; Rousselot-funded). DOI 10.1002/fsn3.4538. ↩↩↩

  15. Reynolds A, et al. (2025) Br J Nutr. Bovine collagen peptides + GLP-1/appetite, n=15 (Rousselot-linked; the glucose effect is still not independently replicated). DOI 10.1017/S0007114525103851. ↩

  16. Kumar P, et al. (2023) J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. GlyNAC (glycine + NAC) RCT, n=24 older adults; single-lab, needs cysteine collagen lacks. DOI 10.1093/gerona/glac135 · PMID 35975308. ↩↩↩

  17. König D, et al. (2018) Nutrients 10(1):97. Specific collagen peptides + bone mineral density in postmenopausal women, n=131, 12 mo (Gelita-funded). DOI 10.3390/nu10010097 · PMID 29337906. ↩↩↩

  18. Jerger S, et al. (2023) Eur J Sport Sci. 5 g collagen peptides + resistance training; tendon CSA increased, stiffness/strength did not (TENDOFORTE/GELITA). DOI 10.1080/17461391.2023.2232758. ↩↩↩

  19. Systematic review of collagen + tendon adaptation (2026) J Funct Morphol Kinesiol 11(1):130. GRADE A that CSA/stiffness rise with training; GRADE A against an added strength effect. DOI 10.3390/jfmk11010130. ↩

  20. Bischof K, et al. (2024) Sports Med. Collagen + resistance-training meta-analysis (19 RCTs / 768); fat-free-mass SMD 0.48, strength SMD 0.19. DOI 10.1007/s40279-024-02079-0 · PMID 39060741. ↩

  21. Sugihara F, et al. (2018) Sci Rep. Collagen-dipeptide dose-response in pressure-ulcer healing, n=112 (Nitta-funded). DOI 10.1038/s41598-018-29831-7 · PMID 30061579. ↩↩

  22. Lee SK, et al. (2006) Adv Skin Wound Care 19(2). Independent pressure-ulcer RCT (n=71); ~2× faster healing vs control. DOI 10.1097/00129334-200603000-00011 · PMID 16557055. ↩

  23. Sugaya K, et al. (2021) J Complement Integr Med 18(3):617-620. Glycine ~6 g/day in overactive-bladder patients (bladder-reflex mechanism; no parallel control). DOI 10.1515/jcim-2020-0282. ↩↩↩↩↩

  24. Buchanan RW, et al. (CONSIST, 2007) Am J Psychiatry. Adjunctive high-dose glycine showed no benefit (largest trial). DOI 10.1176/appi.ajp.2007.06081358 · PMID 17898352. ↩↩↩

  25. Biswas S, Hilser JR, et al. (2023-24, Hazen lab) pleiotropy-robust Mendelian randomization + ApoE⁻/⁻ mouse feeding: no causal glycine→atherosclerosis effect. DOI 10.1101/2023.12.08.23299748 · PMID 38168321 (preprint). ↩↩↩

  26. Clean Label Project (2024) protein-powder heavy-metal report — ~47% exceeded a Prop-65 threshold; brand-variable. ↩↩

  27. Knight J, et al. (2006) Kidney Int 70(11). Hydroxyproline/gelatin raises urinary oxalate (+43% vs whey); glycine itself <5% of urinary oxalate. PMID 17021603. ↩↩

docs.title: Creatine: What It Actually Does vs What People Think
docs.text:
Creatine is the most-studied and best-selling sports supplement on earth — and the most myth-laden. It is blamed for kidney damage, hair loss, "being a steroid," permanent bloating and cramps, and sold in a dozen "advanced" forms. What does it actually do, which claims (good and bad) survive the peer-reviewed evidence, and how should a healthy person use it? This is the whole picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence backs each claim — settled consensus first, single-study and myth last.

Complete · Published 23 July 2026 · Every one of the 119 cited studies was title-matched against PubMed (0 wrong, 0 retracted) and the load-bearing ones read from source · 119 cited sources · Confidence: High on efficacy-with-training, the kidney-artifact, safety, and "not a steroid"; Moderate–High on the hair-loss myth; Moderate on the therapeutic claims (depression, glycemic, bone, long-COVID).

TL;DR

Creatine monohydrate is the most-studied, best-evidenced and safest sports supplement, and almost every scary claim about it is wrong. Taken daily and paired with resistance training it genuinely builds strength and lean mass,12 though the fast early gain is partly intracellular water1312 and it does almost nothing for muscle without training.1 The "it wrecks your kidneys" fear is a measurement artifact — creatine raises serum creatinine without lowering true kidney filtration1416 — and the scare traces to two confounded case reports.2223 It does not cause dehydration or cramps (if anything, fewer),3032 it is not a steroid and does not change testosterone,34 and the hair-loss panic rests on one 2009 study that never measured hair36 — the only trial that did measure hair was null.38 You do not need to load or cycle it,39 and plain monohydrate beats every "advanced" form.41424344 Brain, mood and metabolic benefits are real but bounded — largest in older adults, vegetarians, the sleep-deprived, or as an antidepressant adjunct in women,464760 and small-to-null in young, rested people.51 The honest caveat cutting the other way: much of the enthusiastic literature is industry-funded — but the safety core is independently corroborated.14285161

The picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence agrees

  • Tier 1 — strongest consensus: creatine + resistance training builds strength and lean mass (nothing without training);12 it does not damage healthy kidneys (the raised creatinine is a metabolic artifact);1416 it is safe long-term across populations;2528 no dehydration or cramps;3032 it is not a steroid and does not change testosterone.34
  • Tier 2 — well-supported, with nuance: the hair-loss scare is one DHT study that never measured hair, and the direct hair trial was null;3638 no loading or cycling needed;39 monohydrate beats every "advanced" form;41 brain benefits are real but bounded to deficit/stress (aging, vegetarians, sleep loss).465147
  • Tier 3 — promising but preliminary: an antidepressant adjunct signal in women;60 glycemic control with exercise only;65 partial bone-geometry benefit;70 an early long-COVID-fatigue signal.68
  • Tier 4 — myths & marketing: "it's a steroid,"35 "wrecks your kidneys,"24 "permanent bloat you can't lose,"12 "you must cycle it,"39 "causes hair loss,"38 "bad for teenagers,"29 "vegetarians don't need it,"9 and the failed cardiac/cancer-cachexia cures.8061

docs.title: What this review covered
docs.text: An eight-part search swept the peer-reviewed literature across the ergogenic core, kidney safety, hormones and hair, brain and mood, women/aging/bone, mechanism and product forms, metabolic and therapeutic claims, and the specific influencer myths (steroid, cycling, bloating, teenagers, vegetarians, contamination). Every candidate PMID — 119 — was then independently title-matched against PubMed (0 wrong, 0 retracted), and the load-bearing abstracts (the efficacy meta-analyses, the kidney meta, the DHT and hair trials, the depression and glycemic RCTs, the metabolic-burden paper, the postmenopausal and cachexia trials) were read from source for their exact numbers. A recurring honesty note runs through it: much of the positive creatine research comes from the industry-linked International Society of Sports Nutrition (ISSN), which is flagged throughout — but the safety core is backed by independent groups.

docs.title: Tier 1 — The strongest evidence (it works, and the safety scares are wrong)
It builds muscle and strength — but the training does most of the work
docs.text: This is the most-replicated result in sports supplementation. Meta-analyses agree that creatine combined with resistance training builds lean mass and strength: a 35-trial meta put the lean-mass gain at +1.10 kg with training (and a non-significant +0.03 kg without exercise),1 a 22-trial meta in older adults found +1.37 kg lean mass plus greater chest- and leg-press strength,23 and strength meta-analyses (upper and lower limb) agree.456 The load-bearing nuance is size and dependency: the pooled body-composition effect is "small-to-moderate,"7 and creatine is an amplifier of resistance training, not a substitute for it — with no training stimulus it does almost nothing.1

docs.title: It is real muscle — but the fast early gain is partly water
docs.text: The "it's just water weight" line is half true. Muscle-biopsy trials show creatine drives genuine growth: larger fiber cross-sectional area,89 more satellite cells and myonuclei (true remodeling),10 and an anabolic gene-expression signature.11 But the rapid first-week increase in scale weight is largely intracellular water drawn into the muscle cell,12 and a 2025 RCT with a supplement wash-in showed that once that water was accounted for, 5 g/day added no lean mass beyond training alone (a +0.51 kg wash-in bump, then no between-group difference).13 So the honest picture is real hypertrophy over months, with the "instant gains" of the first week being cell hydration.

docs.title: The kidney scare is a measurement artifact
docs.text: Creatine is metabolized to creatinine, so supplementing mildly raises serum creatinine — and therefore lowers estimated-GFR equations — without any true fall in kidney filtration.1415 Studies using measured GFR (⁵¹Cr-EDTA and clearance markers) find no renal impairment,1617 including in resistance-trained men on high-protein diets18 and in type-2 diabetics with elevated renal risk;19 three separate meta-analyses reach the same conclusion.142021 The entire "creatine destroys kidneys" narrative traces to two confounded single-patient case reports — both in people with pre-existing kidney disease and on other nephrotoxic drugs.2223 A 2023 review put it bluntly: it is time for a "requiem" for the creatine-kidney-failure myth.24

docs.title: It is safe long-term, across populations
docs.text: Up to 21 months of use left roughly 50 clinical health markers unchanged;25 the ISSN safe ceiling is 30 g/day for five years;26 and high doses were tolerated in months-long trials in ALS27 patients. A dedicated female-only adverse-event meta-analysis (29 studies / 951 women) recorded no deaths and no serious adverse events, and no difference versus placebo in total adverse events, gastrointestinal events, weight gain, or renal/hepatic markers.28 Youth are not a special-risk group either — a review of active adolescents found a strong safety profile and no evidence-based reason for a blanket ban.29

docs.title: No dehydration, no cramps — if anything, fewer
docs.text: The dehydration/cramp myth runs the opposite way to the evidence. A meta-analysis found no impairment of hydration or thermoregulation and no rise in heat-illness risk;30 subjects who exercised dehydrated in the heat were unharmed;31 and season-long football cohorts had fewer cramps, dehydration episodes and injuries on creatine.3233 The intracellular water creatine draws into muscle is, if anything, protective.

docs.title: It is not a steroid and does not raise testosterone
docs.text: Creatine is a naturally occurring amino-acid derivative found in meat and fish — not a hormone, and not a banned substance. Controlled trials show no change versus placebo in total testosterone, SHBG, free-androgen index, cortisol, growth hormone or IGF-1,34 and umbrella reviews of "testosterone boosters" place it outside the category of supplements that raise serum T.35

docs.title: Tier 2 — Well-supported, with real nuance (hair, dosing, forms, brain)
The hair-loss scare rests on one study that never measured hair
docs.text: The entire "creatine causes baldness" claim comes from a single 2009 study in 20 rugby players, in which DHT rose (+56% after loading, +40% after maintenance) and the DHT-to-testosterone ratio rose — but which never measured hair and was never replicated,36 and was formally critiqued at publication.37 In 2025 the first trial designed to look at hair directly — 12 weeks, 5 g/day, with Trichogram and FotoFinder imaging — found no change in DHT, in the DHT:T ratio, or in any hair-density, thickness or follicle-count measure versus placebo, and concluded it was "strong evidence against the claim that creatine contributes to hair loss."38 The mechanism is an unreplicated hypothesis; the only outcome data are null.

docs.title: You do not need to "load," and you do not need to "cycle"
docs.text: A loading phase (20 g/day for about six days) saturates muscle creatine quickly, but 3–5 g/day reaches the identical saturation in three to four weeks — loading only speeds arrival, it is not required.3940 There is likewise no cycling requirement: continuous 3–5 g/day simply maintains the elevated stores, and stopping lets them drift back to baseline over about a month.39

docs.title: Plain monohydrate is the gold standard — the "advanced" forms are marketing
docs.text: Creatine monohydrate is roughly 99% bioavailable and raises muscle stores 15–40%.41 Head-to-head trials show every marketed alternative fails to beat it: creatine ethyl ester was actually worse (lower muscle-creatine gain, more degradation),42 buffered "Kre-Alkalyn" was no better even at a lower dose,43 and creatine nitrate did not outperform equimolar monohydrate.44 Pay for monohydrate, ideally a tested brand.

docs.title: Brain benefits are real but concentrated in deficit and stress
docs.text: Creatine crosses the blood–brain barrier slowly — brain creatine rises only about 8% versus roughly 20% in muscle45 — so the cognitive effect is largest where the brain is short of it or under stress: memory in older adults,46 vegetarians (lower baseline stores),4748 and sleep deprivation.4950 In young, rested, meat-eating adults the largest trial to date (123 people) found only a small, borderline effect and no vegetarian advantage,51 and a balanced review confirms the benefit shrinks toward zero without a deficit.50 The "response" question is the same story from the muscle side: uptake is inversely related to your baseline, so low-store individuals — especially vegetarians — gain the most and already-saturated meat-eaters the least.5253

docs.title: Women benefit and it is safe
docs.text: Women carry roughly 70–80% lower endogenous creatine stores than men.54 The evidence in women is favorable: a 2026 meta-analysis of postmenopausal trials found +0.37 kg lean mass and +7.5 kg leg-press strength when at least 5 g/day is combined with resistance training,55 vulnerable older women improved lean mass and function with creatine plus training,56 and there is no evidence that creatine disrupts female sex hormones.57

docs.title: Tier 3 — Promising but preliminary, or conditional
docs.text:
• Depression — the strongest data are for augmentation: adding 5 g/day to an SSRI accelerated and deepened the response in women with major depression (greater symptom improvement from week 2),60 with a supporting brain-bioenergetics rationale62 and an association in a large dietary survey.63 But this is adjunct-to-medication in women, not a monotherapy, and an open-label study saw both bipolar patients switch into hypomania — a real safety flag.64
  • Blood sugar — creatine plus exercise cut HbA1c by 1.1% in type-2 diabetics and raised GLUT-4 translocation (the strongest human trial),65 but without an exercise co-intervention, meta-analyses find no glycemic effect.6667
  • Fatigue / long-COVID — small, mostly single-blind trials suggest 6 g/day reduces fatigue and raises grip strength;6869 promising, not established.
  • Bone — the best 12-month trial attenuated femoral-neck bone-density loss and improved hip bone geometry with resistance training,70 but overall bone density is unchanged in the meta-analysis55 and reviews decline to recommend creatine as an osteoporosis therapy.7172
  • Immune / inflammation — an acute blunting of post-exertion cytokines,73 but no chronic anti-inflammatory effect (a meta of double-blind trials was null for CRP, IL-6 and TNF).74
  • Brain injury — mechanistically promising, with one small pediatric study,75 but no adult concussion trial.50
  • Caffeine — the "caffeine cancels creatine" rule is a single 1996 study of nine people;76 later reviews find everyday co-use is fine.7778

docs.title: Tier 4 — Myths, misconceptions, and marketing
docs.text:
• "It's a steroid / a banned PED." No — it is an amino-acid derivative from meat and fish, not a hormone, and it is not on any banned list.3526
  • "It wrecks your kidneys." The higher creatinine reading is the metabolic artifact, not lost filtration; measured-GFR studies and meta-analyses show no decline even in kidney patients.1724
  • "Water bloat you can't lose." The water it retains is inside the muscle cell, not subcutaneous, so it does not read as a puffy look, and it reverses within about four weeks of stopping.1239
  • "You must cycle it." There is no physiological basis; continuous 3–5 g/day simply keeps stores saturated.39
  • "It causes hair loss." One DHT study, no hair endpoint, direct hair trial null (see Tier 2).3638
  • "Bad for teenagers." No evidence of harm in active youth.29
  • "Vegetarians don't need it." Backwards — they have lower baseline stores and respond the most.947
  • Cardiac and cancer-cachexia cures. Creatine improved skeletal-muscle endurance but not heart function in heart-failure trials,79 added nothing to cardiac rehabilitation,80 and a 263-patient cancer trial found no benefit for the anorexia/weight-loss syndrome.61 Not every promising clinical use panned out.

docs.title: What actually matters
docs.text:
For a healthy person who lifts, creatine is close to a free lunch: creatine monohydrate, 3–5 g every day, with no loading and no cycling,39 taken consistently and paired with resistance training for the muscle benefit,1 from a third-party-tested product (Informed-Sport / Creapure) because roughly 15% of loosely-regulated supplements have been found contaminated with undeclared substances.58 Expect modest, real strength and lean-mass gains over months (not a dramatic overnight change once the initial water settles), possible cognitive help if you are older, vegetarian or sleep-deprived, and none of the harms the myths promise. What it will not do is build muscle without training, boost testosterone, cure a disease, or wreck your kidneys or hairline.

One mechanistic footnote worth knowing: your body makes creatine from the amino acids glycine, arginine and methionine, and this synthesis consumes a striking ~40% of the body's methyl groups (from methionine/SAM) — which is part of why vegetarians, who must make nearly all their own creatine, respond the most to supplementation.59

Informational only, not medical advice. This page is a public link but is unlisted (noindex). Every citation points to a public, independently verifiable source (DOI / PMID). Available in English and Ελληνικά.

  1. Delpino FM, et al. (2022) Nutrition 103-104:111791. Meta of 35 RCTs / 1,192 people: lean mass +1.10 kg with resistance training, +0.03 kg (NS) without. DOI 10.1016/j.nut.2022.111791 · PMID 35986981. ↩↩↩↩↩↩

  2. Chilibeck PD, et al. (2017) Open Access J Sports Med 8:213-226. Meta of 22 RCTs / 721 older adults: +1.37 kg lean mass with resistance training. DOI 10.2147/OAJSM.S123529 · PMID 29138605. Authors declare no COI. ↩↩↩

  3. Devries MC, Phillips SM (2014) Med Sci Sports Exerc 46(6):1194-1203. Meta, 357 older adults: greater lean-mass/strength gains with creatine + RT. DOI 10.1249/MSS.0000000000000220 · PMID 24576864. ↩

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text: Every research here follows the same rules: cite each claim to a primary source, grade the strength of that source, state confidence honestly, and report facts and uncertainty rather than a tidy verdict. Findings are then presented ranked by how strongly the evidence backs them — settled, multi-study consensus first; single-study or contested claims last — so the reader can see at a glance how much weight each point deserves. This page is the shared key those reports point back to.

docs.title: Confidence levels
docs.text:
Each report states a confidence per question — and often a different confidence for different parts of the same question (exposure can be certain while disease causation is not).

Level What it means High Multiple independent studies agree, ideally with a controlled or quantified design (e.g. a glove-controlled exposure trial). Unlikely to reverse with new data. Moderate A real, consistent signal, but the evidence is mostly observational / cross-sectional, heterogeneous, or genuinely contested between experts. Direction is clearer than magnitude. Low–Moderate Plausible and mechanism-consistent, but not directly demonstrated for the specific claim — inferred from adjacent evidence. Treat as a hypothesis, not a fact.

docs.title: Evidence grades
docs.text:
Every source in a bibliography carries one of these grades, so a reader can see at a glance how much weight a claim deserves.

Grade Source type Weight Agency Official risk assessment or regulation (EFSA, US FDA, ECHA, NTP, EU law). Highest for policy / exposure limits; agencies can still disagree — when they do, the report says so. Systematic review / meta-analysis Pooled, pre-registered synthesis across many primary studies. Highest primary-evidence tier for an effect. Primary human Biomonitoring or controlled human-exposure study. Strong for exposure; for disease, weaker if cross-sectional than if prospective. Secondary Narrative review, expert commentary, or expert testimony. Context and corroboration, not a standalone fact. News Journalism. Orientation only — never the sole support for a claim.

docs.title: How each citation is verified
docs.text:
Automated "deep research" tools are useful for finding sources but can misfire when they try to self-verify. The flow used here treats their output as leads and checks every one by hand:

  1. Harvest leads. Gather candidate claims and sources from searches — treat them as unverified.
  2. Verify against the primary source. Open each study's DOI / PMID / agency page and confirm the exact number or quote before it is published. If it can't be confirmed, it is omitted — never guessed.
  3. Gap-fill. Any thin part of the question gets fresh searches and primary reading, each new source cited.
  4. Grade and write. Only then is the report written, with each claim tied to a graded source.

docs.title: The re-verification pass
docs.text:
Citations are not verified once and trusted forever. Every published report is periodically re-read from its primary sources, and this pass has its own hard rules — because automated tools and even careful notes make two specific, recurring mistakes.

  1. Every identifier is title-matched against the source database. For each cited PMID, the live PubMed record is pulled and its title/author checked against what the report claims — because a study identifier can silently point to the wrong paper (a different study entirely). This has caught real errors: on one re-verification, two citations turned out to carry the ID of an unrelated paper, and were corrected.
  2. Every cited study is read from its own abstract, not just the load-bearing few — so a number, a direction of effect, or a funding tie can be corrected against what the study actually reports.
  3. Corrections are shown, not hidden. When re-verification changes something, the report carries a short "What changed" note listing every fix (a wrong identifier, a reversed finding, an author name), so the reader can see the correction rather than a silently-edited page.

The goal is that a claim's evidence is exactly as strong as it looks — and that when it isn't, the page says so.

Standing rules

  • Never invent a study, author, DOI, or number. Missing or unverifiable → it is left out, not filled in.
  • Cite everything. Every claim traces to a numbered source.
  • Facts, not verdicts. Contested points get the evidence on both sides and an explicit flag of what's genuinely uncertain — industry-funded and null results are weighed, not ignored, and an agency's press position never outranks the primary evidence.
  • Literature-current date. Each report stamps when its evidence was last checked; science moves, and old reports are re-verified before being relied on again.

docs.title: Seed Oils: What They Actually Do vs What People Think
docs.text:
Seed oils — soybean, canola, corn, sunflower, safflower, cottonseed, grapeseed, rice-bran, all high in the omega-6 fat linoleic acid — are blamed by the "Hateful Eight," the "make frying oil tallow again" movement, and carnivore-diet advocates for inflammation, heart disease, obesity, diabetes and cancer. Which of those hold up, how strong is the evidence, and is any of it seed-oil-specific? This is the whole picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence backs each claim — settled consensus first, single-study and myth last.

Complete · Published 20 July 2026 · Expanded the same day with a second literature sweep — every one of the 91 cited studies title-matched against PubMed (0 wrong; 1 retraction caught) and the load-bearing ones read from source · 96 cited sources · Confidence: High on the inflammation-myth and the neutral-to-favorable metabolic picture · Moderate on the cardiovascular benefit (events yes, mortality unclear) and the frying-oxidation concern.

TL;DR

For a healthy person eating whole foods, seed oils used in normal cooking are not the chronic-disease driver the popular narrative claims. The central mechanism — omega-6 linoleic acid (LA) causing inflammation — fails in humans: eating more LA does not raise tissue arachidonic acid,1 and does not raise CRP, IL-6 or TNF in randomized trials.23 Across large studies, higher LA is consistently tied to lower heart disease,5 lower type-2 diabetes,67 and lower death;89 controlled feeding trials show swapping saturated fat for PUFA improves insulin sensitivity and lowers liver fat131416 — the opposite of "LA makes you fat." There is one legitimate concern: repeatedly heated / deep-fried oil generates toxic aldehydes (real chemistry),25 but no study links that to human disease at real intakes, and fried-food harm is "largely mediated by body weight."28 The honest cardiovascular nuance: replacing saturated fat with omega-6 lowers cholesterol and events but not clearly mortality, and omega-6 alone (no omega-3) is the weakest case.24 Myths: "all seed oils are the same" (canola is ~19% LA and carries omega-3),39 "seed oils cause cancer" (the one trial is confounded; meta-analyses are null),4041 "swap back to tallow" (raises LDL),19 and conflating today's oils with the industrial trans fat that was actually banned in 2018.42

The picture, ranked by how strongly the evidence agrees

  • Tier 1 — strongest consensus: the omega-6→inflammation mechanism fails in humans;12 higher LA → lower CVD, diabetes and mortality (biomarker cohorts + Mendelian randomization);56810 feeding trials favour PUFA over saturated fat;1314 the omega-6:omega-3 "ratio" is an artifact (both PUFAs are protective).12
  • Tier 2 — well-supported, with nuance: replacing saturated fat with PUFA lowers cholesterol + events but not clearly mortality, and omega-6-alone is the weak case;211924 heated/deep-fried oil makes toxic aldehydes (real chemistry, human disease unproven);25 fried-food harm tracks body weight.28
  • Tier 3 — weak / single-study / mechanism: the "LA→obesity via mitochondria" theory is mouse + hypothesis;3032 tissue LA really rose but that's exposure, not proof;33 the skin-cancer link is weak and confounded (no human sunburn data at all).35
  • Tier 4 — myths & marketing: "all seed oils are the same,"39 "cause cancer,"40 "swap to beef tallow,"19 and "these are the vegetable oils we already knew were bad" (that was trans fat).42

docs.title: What the deeper search added
docs.text:
A second, wider literature sweep (32 more sources, → 96) did not overturn the verdict — it hardened it and added five refinements:

  • The biggest one: "linoleic acid" is not the same as "omega-6." It is linoleic acid specifically that tracks with lower disease, while the other omega-6 fats (γ-linolenic, dihomo-γ-linolenic, arachidonic) often go the opposite way — adipose arachidonic acid → higher mortality,43 the other n-6 fats → higher diabetes,44 and non-linoleic omega-6 → higher dementia and higher body fat.4546 "Omega-6 is harmful" wrongly lumps a protective fat with harmful ones.
  • Fresh, industry-free mortality data reinforce it. A Danish study found body-fat linoleic acid → 24% lower death,43 and a 221,054-person US study found swapping 10 g/day of butter for plant oils → 17% lower total and cancer mortality;47 a 2026 trial testing the social-media claim directly again found no effect of soybean oil on inflammation or oxidized LDL.48
  • The best-evidenced fat is olive oil / the Mediterranean pattern — which does not make seed oils toxic. The strongest outcome trials are for the Mediterranean diet with extra-virgin olive oil (PREDIMED, 31% fewer cardiovascular events; its 2013 report was retracted for randomization flaws and re-analyzed in 2018 with the same result)49 and CORDIOPREV;50 meanwhile the proposed replacements butter and coconut oil raise LDL,5152 and canola oil improved blood sugar in a diabetes trial.53 "Prefer olive oil" is well-supported; "seed oils are poison" is not.
  • Brain, dementia and body weight run opposite to the scare. Higher blood linoleic acid → lower dementia,45 higher omega-6 → lower dementia,54 and higher circulating linoleic acid → smaller waist, lower weight and less body fat in 272,587 people.46
  • Two honest caveats got sharper. The inflammation question is genuinely contested at the observational level (one 2025 independent study found "no simple pro-/anti-inflammatory dichotomy")55 even though every randomized trial stays null; there is now one real positive cancer signal — a 2025 review found dietary-questionnaire linoleic acid → 15% higher colorectal cancer, though tissue-biomarker linoleic acid was null;56 and pregnancy and infancy are the one window where the omega-6:omega-3 balance genuinely matters more (rising formula/breast-milk linoleic acid may crowd out the DHA and arachidonic acid a developing brain needs)5758 — a caveat about infants, not adults cooking with oil. The one direct human test of repeatedly-heated frying oil found no acute harm to blood-vessel function.59

docs.title: Tier 1 — The strongest evidence (the mechanism fails; the picture is neutral-to-favorable)
The inflammation mechanism breaks at step one in humans
docs.text: The whole theory is a chain: seed oils are high in linoleic acid → LA converts to arachidonic acid → arachidonic acid drives inflammation → disease. It is textbook biochemistry, and it fails at its very first step in people. A systematic review of human trials found that increasing dietary LA up to six-fold did not raise arachidonic acid in blood, and cutting LA by up to 90% did nothing either — while feeding arachidonic acid directly did raise it, proving the method works.1 Downstream, two independent reviews of randomized trials — 30 trials / 1,377 people2 and 15 trials in healthy people3 — found no effect of higher LA on CRP, IL-6, TNF or other inflammatory markers, and a feeding trial stated it plainly: "a high n-6 PUFA intake does not cause any signs of inflammation or oxidative stress."4 The single asterisk is a possible CRP rise only at the very largest LA increases, not significant in the pooled data.2

docs.title: Higher linoleic acid tracks with less disease, not more
docs.text: If seed oils were driving disease, people with more LA in their bodies should be sicker. They are not. The largest biomarker analysis — 30 cohorts, 68,659 people — found higher circulating LA tied to lower total cardiovascular disease, lower cardiovascular death, and lower stroke; even arachidonic acid was not harmful.5 For diabetes, a pooled analysis of 20 cohorts found higher LA → 35% lower risk,6 and a dose-response meta of 31 cohorts found risk falling to the highest intakes.7 For death, a meta of 44 cohorts / 811,069 people found higher LA → lower all-cause, cardiovascular and cancer mortality,8 and the Nurses'/Health-Professionals cohorts found replacing 5%-energy saturated fat with PUFA → 27% lower total mortality.9 Mendelian randomization — genetics used to approximate a trial — supports a causal LA→lower-diabetes effect,10 and a second MR is consistent with high circulating LA being protective.11 The honest caveat: two flagship pooled analyses were part-funded by Unilever, which sells vegetable-oil spreads56 — which is exactly why the industry-free corroboration (the mortality meta,8 UK Biobank,12 the MR10) matters, and it points the same way.

docs.title: In feeding trials, the fat you're told to eat instead is the worse one
docs.text: The cleanest test is a controlled feeding trial. A meta-analysis of 102 randomized feeding trials found that replacing saturated fat (or carbohydrate) with PUFA lowered glucose, HbA1c and insulin resistance — "most consistent favourable effects seen with PUFA."13 Overfeeding studies are sharper still: at identical weight gain, saturated fat caused markedly more liver fat and about twice the belly-fat gain of PUFA, while PUFA produced about three times more lean tissue;14 a second trial found saturated fat +50% liver fat with rising ceramides while PUFA raised neither;15 a third found liver-fat gain of saturated +55% > carbs +33% > unsaturated +15%, with insulin resistance only on saturated fat.16 Swapping seed oils for butter, tallow or coconut oil moves you toward the fat these trials show is metabolically worse.

docs.title: The omega-6:omega-3 "ratio" is the wrong metric — and the scary ratio study is a mirage
docs.text: The ratio hypothesis is the backbone of the panic, but a critical appraisal argues it is outdated — a ratio hides the individual levels that matter, and the real Western-diet deficit is low omega-3, not high omega-6.17 The most-cited "proof" that the ratio kills — a UK Biobank study of 85,425 people — is routinely mis-read: yes, a high omega-6/omega-3 ratio tracked 26% higher mortality, but in the same cohort both omega-6 and omega-3 were individually protective (the highest omega-6 group had 23% lower all-cause death).12 The ratio only looks bad because omega-3 protects more strongly. "High ratio → death" is not "omega-6 is harmful."

docs.title: Tier 2 — Well-supported, with real nuance (the honest CVD story + the one legitimate concern)
Cardiovascular disease: cholesterol and events improve, mortality is unclear, and omega-6-alone is the weak case
docs.text: This is where the skeptics have their strongest footing, and it deserves an honest hearing. Replacing saturated fat with PUFA reliably lowers LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular events — 19% fewer coronary events in one RCT meta,18 21% fewer cardiovascular events in another,19 and the American Heart Association's advisory puts the reduction near 30%.20 But that benefit does not clearly translate to living longer: the same reviews find no change in all-cause or cardiovascular mortality,19 and the omega-6-specific Cochrane review found no benefit beyond a possible reduction in heart attacks.21 The two recovered-data trials the anti-seed-oil camp cites are real and NIH-funded: the Sydney Diet Heart Study (safflower oil → higher mortality)22 and the Minnesota Coronary Experiment (corn oil lowered cholesterol but not death).23 But both used pure omega-6 with no omega-3, and the Sydney margarine likely carried trans fat. Ramsden's own meta is the reconciliation: mixed omega-3+omega-6 diets cut heart events 22%, while omega-6-specific diets increased risk 13%.24 Seed oils are the omega-6-alone bucket — not proven cardioprotective on their own, but the "they cause heart disease" claim is contradicted by the biomarker and mortality data.

docs.title: Heating and frying: the one legitimate concern — real chemistry, unproven human magnitude
docs.text: Here the skeptics are right about the chemistry. Frying PUFA-rich oil to high temperature generates cytotoxic and genotoxic aldehydes (4-HNE, acrolein) that penetrate the food, and it gets worse with repeated reuse.25 Dietary LA also raises circulating oxidized-LA metabolites in humans.26 If there is a real case against seed oils, it lives here. But the case stops at chemistry: the field's own leading skeptic, in the very review documenting the aldehydes, concludes there is an "urgent and unmet clinical need" for the trials that would connect them to human disease — i.e. those outcome studies do not yet exist.25 The alarming physiological data are animal (repeatedly-heated oil raised blood pressure in rats — single lab, six animals per group),27 and the human fried-food cohorts, while real, are "largely mediated by body weight and comorbid hypertension and hypercholesterolemia"28 — a cohort meta found fried food raised cardiovascular events but not all-cause or cardiovascular mortality.29 Two nuances matter: the danger is about reuse and thermal abuse (industrial deep-frying) more than a home sauté, and oxidized-LA metabolites in your blood mostly reflect how much LA you eat, not "you ate rancid oil"26 — "avoid oxidized oil" and "eat less LA" are different claims the literature keeps conflating.

docs.title: Tier 3 — Weaker, single-study, mechanism, or conditional
The "LA makes you fat via mitochondria" theory is mouse and hypothesis
docs.text: The obesity thesis has an appealing mechanism and essentially no human support. It rests on mouse studies — soybean oil more obesogenic than coconut oil or fructose,30 LA raising endocannabinoids to induce obesity31 — and hypothesis papers.32 The mouse mechanism that is shown is endocannabinoid, not the mitochondrial-ROS story usually invoked, and 40%-fat mouse diets don't model human eating. Against it stand the human feeding trials, which show PUFA producing less fat and more lean than saturated fat. When mouse mechanism and human trial disagree, the human trial wins.

docs.title: Tissue linoleic acid really has risen — but that is exposure, not proof of harm
docs.text: The consumption story is true: US body-fat LA rose 136% over half a century,33 tracking a more-than-1,000-fold rise in soybean-oil intake across the century.34 But this is the exposure trend, not causation — the same century added calories, sugar, ultra-processed food and sedentism, and the cohorts show higher LA tracking with better outcomes. A rising exposure curve over a rising disease curve is a hypothesis, not a finding.

docs.title: Skin, gut and processing: small, confounded, or mixed
docs.text: The "seed oils cause sunburn" claim has zero human evidence — it is mechanism and marketing. There is a weak, confounded cohort link between PUFA intake and non-melanoma skin cancer over decades,35 not sunburn. For inflammatory disease the evidence mostly points the other way — higher LA was tied to 71% lower rheumatoid-arthritis risk36 — with a genuine but weak harm signal only for ulcerative colitis. And the "seed oils rot your brain" claim is contradicted by data tying circulating LA/arachidonic acid to slower cognitive decline.37 On processing: seed oils are hexane-extracted and refined, the EU caps residue in finished oil at 1 mg/kg, and no evidence links dietary hexane residue to harm — the honest caveat is that the safety assessment is old and monitoring data are sparse,38 so neither "toxic" nor "definitively safe" is fully earned on residues.

docs.title: Tier 4 — Myths, misconceptions, and marketing
"All seed oils are the same"
docs.text: False. Linoleic-acid content runs from ~75% (safflower) down to ~19% (canola) — and canola also carries ~9% omega-3 ALA, making its profile closer to olive oil than to corn oil; high-oleic sunflower and safflower are ~13-20% LA.39 The seed's name is not the hazard.

docs.title: "Seed oils cause the obesity / chronic-disease epidemic"
docs.text: Monocausal and contradicted. The ecological correlation is confounded, the mechanism is mouse and hypothesis, and the human biomarker, feeding-trial and mortality data run neutral-to-favorable. No credible human evidence supports "seed oils are the cause."

docs.title: "Higher linoleic acid causes heart disease"
docs.text: The kernel of truth is real — LA is the main PUFA in LDL, and oxidized LDL is atherogenic — but the leap fails at the outcome: higher circulating LA is associated with lower cardiovascular disease and mortality.58 Digestion does not route dietary LA into arterial plaque.

docs.title: "Seed oils cause cancer"
docs.text: The one human trial signal (a 1971 corn-oil trial with more cancer) had a control group with roughly twice the heavy smokers and was disclaimed by its own authors;40 a later meta of LA and cancer was essentially null,41 and the cohorts tie LA to lower cancer mortality.8 Not supported.

docs.title: "Swap back to beef tallow / butter / coconut oil"
docs.text: Not supported for health outcomes. Tallow (~50% saturated fat) and butter (~63%) raise LDL versus seed oils, and no outcome trial supports the swap.19 The single defensible point is narrow — a more saturated fat oxidizes less on repeated high-heat frying — a cooking-stability argument, not a cardiovascular-benefit one.

docs.title: "These are the same vegetable oils we already knew were bad"
docs.text: No. The genuinely harmful "vegetable oil" was partially hydrogenated oil — industrial trans fat — declared not-safe and removed from the US food supply by 2018.42 Today's non-hydrogenated seed oils are a chemically different product; conflating them is a core error in the anti-seed-oil narrative.

docs.title: Scorecard — how the popular claims hold up
docs.text: Claim ("Hateful Eight" / MAHA / carnivore) Verdict Tier Seed oils cause inflammation (omega-6 → arachidonic acid) Myth (fails in humans)<sup>1</sup><sup>2</sup> 1 Higher linoleic acid causes heart disease Contradicted (LA → lower CVD)<sup>5</sup><sup>8</sup> 1 Seed oils cause diabetes / insulin resistance Contradicted (LA → lower T2D; PUFA improves glycemia)<sup>6</sup><sup>13</sup> 1 Seed oils make you fat (mitochondrial ROS) Unsupported (mouse/hypothesis; human trials opposite)<sup>14</sup><sup>30</sup> 1/3 High omega-6:omega-3 ratio is harmful Overstated (artifact; both PUFAs protective)<sup>12</sup> 1 Replacing saturated fat with seed oil is heart-healthy Partly true (events + LDL down; mortality unclear)<sup>18</sup><sup>19</sup><sup>24</sup> 2 Heated/deep-fried seed oil makes toxic aldehydes True (chemistry) — human disease unproven<sup>25</sup> 2 Fried food is unhealthy True — but via body weight, not the oil<sup>28</sup><sup>29</sup> 2 Hexane processing makes seed oils toxic Overstated (residues ≤1 ppm; data old)<sup>38</sup> 3 Seed oils cause sunburn / skin damage Unsupported (no human sunburn data)<sup>35</sup> 3 All seed oils are the same False (canola ~19% LA + omega-3)<sup>39</sup> 4 Seed oils cause cancer Myth (1971 trial confounded; metas null)<sup>40</sup><sup>41</sup> 4 Swap back to beef tallow / butter Not supported for CVD (raises LDL)<sup>19</sup> 4 Seed oils = the vegetable-oil harm we knew False (that was industrial trans fat, banned 2018)<sup>42</sup> 4 "Omega-6 is one thing" False (linoleic acid protective; other n-6 fats diverge/harmful)<sup>43</sup><sup>44</sup><sup>46</sup> 1 Prefer olive oil / Mediterranean Confirmed (best-evidenced fat — but ≠ seed oils toxic)<sup>49</sup><sup>50</sup><sup>60</sup> 2 Seed oils rot your brain / cause dementia Contradicted (higher linoleic acid → lower dementia)<sup>45</sup><sup>54</sup> 2 Seed oils make you fat Contradicted (higher linoleic acid → lower weight/waist/fat)<sup>46</sup> 1 Seed oils cause colorectal cancer Weak (dietary-questionnaire signal only; biomarker null)<sup>56</sup> 4 Omega-6 balance matters for infants Partly true (the one balance-sensitive window)<sup>57</sup><sup>58</sup> 3

docs.title: The honest seed-oil checklist
docs.text:
1. Don't fear seed oils in normal home cooking — the inflammation mechanism fails in humans and the metabolic and mortality data are neutral-to-favorable versus saturated fat.1813
  2. The real target is deep-fried and ultra-processed food, which is unhealthy largely through calories and body weight, not the specific oil.2829
  3. For repeated high-heat frying, prefer more stable fats (olive, avocado, or a more saturated fat) — this is where the oxidation chemistry is legitimate.25
  4. Don't swap to tallow or butter for your heart — they raise LDL, and no outcome trial supports it.19
  5. Get enough omega-3 (fish / EPA+DHA) — the real Western-diet deficit is low omega-3, so "fixing the ratio" means adding omega-3, not fearing omega-6.1712
  6. Body fat and total diet dwarf oil choice.

This is informational, not medical advice.

docs.title: How this research was done
docs.text:
Seven independent research passes swept the claim space (definitions and the inflammation mechanism, cardiovascular trials, oxidation and frying, metabolic and obesity, biomarker cohorts and Mendelian randomization, cancer/skin/mortality, and the specific oils/history/processing claims), then a second eight-angle sweep deepened it (newest 2023-26 evidence, mental-health and neurology, the recommended alternatives, pregnancy and infancy, oxidation mechanism, gut and autoimmune, appetite and weight, and the influencer/regulatory claims). Every one of the 91 study identifiers was title-matched against PubMed (0 wrong; one retracted trial caught and replaced with its republication) and the load-bearing studies read from their source abstracts, with funding ties flagged throughout. See the methodology for how evidence is graded and verified.

  1. Rett BS, Whelan J (2011) Nutr Metab (Lond) 8:36. Increasing dietary linoleic acid up to 6-fold did not raise tissue arachidonic acid in adults; the mechanism fails at step one. DOI 10.1186/1743-7075-8-36 · PMID 21663641. ↩↩↩↩↩

  2. Su H, et al. (2017) Food Funct 8(9):3091-3103. Meta-analysis, 30 RCTs / 1,377 people: higher LA had no significant effect on TNF-α, IL-6, CRP, adiponectin or MCP-1. DOI 10.1039/c7fo00433h · PMID 28752873. ↩↩↩↩↩
